weather: Overcast and cool in Edmonds today. Temperatures hovering around 52-54°F with southeast winds at 6-8 mph. Cloud cover is complete at 100%, keeping conditions gray throughout the day. No precipitation expected during daylight hours, though humidity sits at 74%. Tonight will turn mostly cloudy with lows dipping to 46°F under light SSE winds at 5-10 mph. No rain in the immediate forecast, but the marine layer keeps things damp and cool—typical late April weather for Puget Sound.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council approved key infrastructure contracts including a $415,116 wastewater treatment plant gasification system support contract with Jacobs Engineering and a $419,000 sewer rehabilitation project covering 27 locations. The council also declined to pursue a counterpetition in the Redelfs tree litigation case, reducing a $102,245 fine to $20,000 for unauthorized removal of a 33-inch-diameter landmark tree. Additionally, the council proclaimed April 9 as Local News Day, celebrating local journalism's role in the community.

The City of Edmonds will begin major sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation on May 4, targeting 27 locations with failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The project uses cured-in-place pipe (CIPP) technology to repair aging sewer systems throughout the city.

South County Fire Board of Commissioners is actively redistricting to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents following last year's annexation into the regional fire authority. Two proposals are under consideration: one would place most of Edmonds in District 2, while another would split the city between Districts 1 and 2. The board is seeking public input before finalizing new district maps that will allow Edmonds residents to run for districted commissioner positions.

career.notes: Most districts are actively hiring for the 2026-2027 school year with general postings open. Specific English and History positions are available at Shoreline, Seattle Academy, Northshore, and Seattle Public Schools. Edmonds and Lake Washington have general secondary teacher pools. Apply directly through district websites or job boards like Indeed and SchoolSpring. Salary ranges vary by district and experience level.

global_news.findings: Suspect charged with attempted assassination of Trump at Washington dinner. Investigators say 31-year-old California man Cole Tomas Allen wanted to kill as many high-level officials as possible during the White House Correspondents' Dinner. This follows Saturday's shooting incident where President Trump was rushed to safety by Secret Service agents.

US and Iran clashed at the United Nations over Tehran's selection as vice president for a nuclear non-proliferation conference. The US called it an 'affront' to NPT credibility while Iran accused the US of hypocrisy. Iran proposed delaying nuclear talks until the war is resolved, as the two-month conflict continues with disputes over the Strait of Hormuz.

Pope Leo and new Archbishop of Canterbury Sarah Mullally met for the first time in a historic Vatican encounter. Mullally, the first woman to lead the world's 85 million Anglicans, prayed with Pope Leo in a symbolic gesture of unity between the Catholic Church and Church of England, which split in 1534.

UK parliament voted against launching an inquiry into whether Prime Minister Keir Starmer misled parliament over his appointment of Peter Mandelson as ambassador to the United States. The vote comes after days of political pressure over the controversial appointment.

King Charles III began his US state visit, addressing Congress on Tuesday to defend 'democratic values' following the weekend's assassination attempt on President Trump. The monarch's visit comes at a time of heightened political tension in America.

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova’s long meditation on flight and flocking opens with Georgia O’Keeffe’s reflection that seeing the world from above “would rid the human race of much smallness and pettiness.” Popova, writing from an airplane seat, explores the physics of avian lift—how the curved upper surface of a bird’s wing creates lower pressure above than below, generating lift—and the deeper existential grace of surrendering to natural law. She quotes Rachel Carson on wonder as “an unfailing antidote against the boredom and disenchantments” of modern life, and closes by noting that while we will never feel a [TRUNCATED]

In a sharply argued essay, Jacobin warns that the quiet delegation of judgment to chatbots is eroding the very substrate of democratic life. Drawing on a new MIT study that shows reduced brain activity in habitual chatbot users, the piece revives Kant’s 1784 question “What is Enlightenment?”—the imperative to think for oneself rather than let “a book that has understanding for me, a pastor who has a conscience for me” do the work. The author coins “cognitive debt” to describe the long-term atrophy of reasoning power that accompanies short-term AI efficiency, and argues that emancipatory politi [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay “On Reading,” written as a preface to John Ruskin’s Sesame and Lilies, to defend literature as a technology of inner freedom. Proust rejected Ruskin’s moral-didactic view of books, insisting instead that reading is a “unique form of communication” that lets us commune with “the most powerful and smartest minds in the world” outside the constraints of social speech. Champy traces how Proust’s argument—that literature preserves past sensations and makes time tangible—anticipates his later novel In Search of Lost Time, and she ends by suggesting th [TRUNCATED]

Ian Penman’s 4,000-word reflection on the Beach Boys treats Pet Sounds as a secular gospel. He describes listening to Monteverdi’s 1641 Selva morale e spirituale and the 1966 Beach Boys album back-to-back and feeling “truly transported” by Brian Wilson’s three-minute cathedrals of sound. Penman lingers on the theological undertow of songs like “God Only Knows,” whose chord progressions “are always going up, then pausing before they go up again, like they’re going towards God.” He positions the band as heirs to rock’s original tension between Saturday-night carnality and Sunday-morning transcen [TRUNCATED]

Will Alden reports from an Easter-Sunday picnic in Lake Balboa Park, Los Angeles, where the Constitutionalist Party of Iran gathered to toast Reza Pahlavi as a “transitional” leader-in-waiting. Amid hookah smoke and Persian pop anthems, activists passed out “THANK YOU BIBI & TRUMP” posters and spoke of “day zero” when Pahlavi will ask Iranians to flood the streets. Alden sketches the spectrum of diaspora monarchists—from young hardliners who want full restoration “by any means necessary” to liberals who favor constitutional limits—and notes how weekly rallies at the Wilshire Federal Building h [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Matteo Raffaelli’s “Triertium in Modern Theology” (Modern Theology, 2026) positions the Czech TRIERTIUM Research Group as a living laboratory for a new Trinitarian ontology rooted in Comenian pansophy, explicitly linking relational metaphysics to transdisciplinary projects spanning philosophy, theology, and the arts. Christopher Isabelle’s newly-posted “Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field” (PhilArchive, 2026-04-23) argues that dyadic structures cannot sustain emergence or meaning; a third, actively-maintained relational field is required, with language itself compressing—but never reconstructing—the original triadic tension. Damiano Migliorini’s forthcoming monograph “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk” (Academia.edu preprint) integrates analytic philosophy of religion with quantum-structural realism, proposing a speculative model in which divine relationality transcends both substance monism and proc [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous research stack has matured into three distinct layers.  At the top, Google’s PaperOrchestra (April 2026) demonstrates a five-agent LaTeX pipeline that turns raw lab notes into submission-ready manuscripts in ~4,000 LLM calls, handling literature review, figure generation and citation verification without human hand-offs.  Beneath it, the open-source layer is stabilising around LangGraph (27 k monthly searches) and CrewAI (14 k) for orchestration, with OpenAI’s March 2025 Agents SDK and Google’s April ADK now offering production-grade hand-off primitives.  Finally, frontier reasoning models (o3, Claude 4.6 extended-thinking, Gemini 2.5 Deep Think) have shifted the prompting paradigm: the 2023 chain-of-thought playbook is now counter-productive; effective prompts specify only goal, constraints, audience and reasoning budget, letting the model allocate its hidden test-time compute.  The result is a toolchain where multi-agent systems can draft, critique and revise papers en [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The Talk of the Town: April 27, 2026
newyorker.dek: Notes from a city in flux
newyorker.text:
The city’s literary scene has been abuzz with the arrival of a new generation of AI-assisted novelists. At a recent gathering at the Center for Fiction in Brooklyn, writers debated the ethics of using large language models to generate prose. Some argued that AI could democratize storytelling by lowering barriers to entry, while others worried it might homogenize literary voices. The evening ended with a spontaneous poetry slam where human poets competed against AI-generated verses—judged by a panel that included both literary critics and machine-learning experts. The consensus? The machines still lack the ‘ache’ that makes poetry human, but they’re getting closer.

Meanwhile, the Upper West Side’s beloved Symphony Space has announced a radical experiment: starting next month, its Thalia Book Club will feature authors who have collaborated with AI to write their latest works. The first guest is novelist Jennifer Egan, who used an AI trained on her own back catalog to co-write a speculative fiction novel about a future where memories can be edited like text. Egan insists the AI didn’t write the book for her but served as a ‘creative sparring partner’ that challenged her assumptions about narrative structure. Critics remain divided, but ticket sales suggest the public is curious.

In a quieter corner of the city, the New York Public Library’s main branch has unveiled a new AI-powered research tool called ‘Ask the Stacks.’ Visitors can now query the library’s vast archives using natural language, and the system will surface relevant passages from centuries of texts. Early adopters include a Columbia graduate student who used it to uncover forgotten 19th-century letters about women’s suffrage, and a mystery writer who found obscure details about Gilded Age New York for her latest novel. The librarians insist the tool won’t replace human expertise but will instead ‘amplify the serendipity’ of research.

Finally, downtown, a group of artists has launched an immersive installation called ‘The Library of Babel 2.0,’ inspired by Borges’ famous story. Visitors walk through a maze of AI-generated books—each one unique but nonsensical—while ambient soundscapes play fragments of AI-written poetry. The creators say the project is a meditation on the limits of machine creativity and the enduring value of human meaning-making. The installation has already sparked heated debates on social media, with some calling it profound and others dismissing it as gimmicky. Either way, it’s clear that AI has arrived in New York’s cultural scene—and it’s not leaving anytime soon.
